_article: XXXIX.2 — rivers continue to erode. XXXIX.1 — the canonical example: 'The bridge collapsed' (history) vs 'The village is slowly dying because trade never recovered' (momentum). The bridge collapse is a river_erosion threshold crossing; the village dying is the downstream consequence in settlement/economy momentum.
_description: Momentum schema for a River entity. A river is not a static water feature. It erodes its banks, shifts course over seasons, deposits spirit minerals where it slows, floods in spring, and threatens bridges and fords. The bridge that collapsed in Wang Lin's fourteenth year collapsed because the river's erosion trajectory had been weakening its abutments for years. This schema declares that trajectory.

_design_notes.why_this_matters: The user explicitly named rivers eroding as a momentum example, and named the bridge-collapse consequence as the canonical history-vs-momentum distinction. This schema makes the river a live geological agent. The bridge is a separate entity (a chokepoint on the travel graph); when the river's erosion crosses the bridge's failure threshold, the bridge collapses — and the village's trade trajectory begins its decline.
_design_notes.coupling: Feeds: hydrology_graph (course drift, floodplain expansion), travel_graph (bridge/chokepoint integrity), settlement_graph (flood risk, trade-route viability), economy_momentum (trade disruption). Fed by: season (spring flood), spirit_vein_momentum (spirit-mineral deposition where the river crosses a vein).
perceivable_without_notification_example: The bridge's stone abutments are cracked and mossy — the river has been working on them for years.
The river is wider than the village map suggests — it has eroded its banks this season.
A ford that was safe last year is now knee-deep — the course has shifted.
Spirit-mineral deposits glitter in a river bend where the water slows — a cultivator notices.

schema.river_id: string — matches a River entity id

current_state.bank_erosion_rate: float — blocks/year at the most eroded bend (driven by flow, soil, season)
current_state.spirit_mineral_deposition_sites: array — {bend_id, deposition_rate, vein_id_coupling} — where the river slows over a spirit vein and deposits minerals
current_state.threatened_chokepoints: array — {chokepoint_id, integrity 0-1, failure_eta_ticks} — bridges/fords the river is currently undermining
current_state.deposited_fertility_sites: array — {floodplain_id, fertility_gain_per_flood} — where floods deposit silt (a positive consequence)

rate_of_change.bank_erosion_per_tick: float — driven by flow_class, soil_type, season (spring flood multiplies), vegetation (roots slow erosion)
rate_of_change.course_shift_per_tick: float — slow drift in meandering sections; sudden in avulsion
rate_of_change.chokepoint_integrity_per_tick: float — negative where the river undermines a bridge/ford

components.flow_force: float — volume * speed; rises in spring, after storms, when tributaries swell
components.soil_resistance: float — bedrock high, clay moderate, sand low
components.vegetation_anchor: float — root systems slow bank erosion (a deforested bank erodes faster)
components.seasonal_flood_multiplier: float — spring = high, summer = low, autumn = moderate, winter = low (ice)

drivers: string — e.g. 'zhao_river: course shifting at the south bend (bank_erosion 0.04 blocks/year), threatens zhao_river_bridge (integrity 0.38, failure_eta ~6000 ticks if no repair), spring flood risk high, deposits spirit minerals at the west bend over spirit_vein_zhao_west (a cultivator's foraging opportunity)'

thresholds.name: chokepoint_failure
thresholds.condition: threatened_chokepoint integrity < 0.2 AND flood event occurs
thresholds.consequence: bridge/ford collapses. This is the canonical history-vs-momentum event: the collapse is history; the downstream trade decline is momentum in settlement/economy schemas. The travel_graph removes the edge; caravans must detour; the supplied settlement enters scarcity risk.
thresholds.irreversible: true for the chokepoint (must be rebuilt)

thresholds.name: course_avulsion
thresholds.consequence: river suddenly changes course; floodplain settlements on the new course are flooded; settlements on the old course lose their water supply; the hydrology_graph and settlement_graph both reshape. This is a canon-grade geological event.

thresholds.name: spring_flood
thresholds.consequence: river floods; floodplains inundated (deposited_fertility_sites gain silt — a positive for agriculture next season); low-lying structures damaged; fords impassable; caravans delayed. A perceivable seasonal rhythm.

thresholds.name: spirit_mineral_bloom
thresholds.condition: spirit_mineral_deposition at a bend crosses harvestable threshold
thresholds.consequence: a cultivator can harvest spirit minerals from the riverbed; this is a perceivable opportunity (glittering deposits visible to spiritual sense); may attract foragers, creating a minor economy trajectory and possibly a conflict trajectory if multiple foragers arrive.

interruptible_by: Bridge repair or reinforcement (raises chokepoint integrity; requires materials and labor — a settlement task)
Bank reinforcement (riprap, vegetation planting; lowers erosion)
Course diversion via formation (canon-grade intervention; may have consequences)
Dam or levee construction (major project; alters flood_risk and downstream flow)
schema.perceivable_without_notification: string — REQUIRED (Art XXXIX.5). e.g. 'The Zhao River bridge's south abutment is cracked through, moss in the fissures, the stone worn by years of current. The river at the south bend has eaten into the bank — the old marker post stands in water now. At the west bend, where the current slows, something glitters in the shallows on a clear day — a cultivator would know it for spirit mineral.' If empty, FAILS the Momentum Test.
schema.compiled_into: hydrology_graph (primary); feeds travel_graph (chokepoint integrity), settlement_graph (flood risk, trade viability), economy_momentum (trade disruption)
schema.canon_ref: INFERRED (Art XV) — rivers and their seasonal behavior are ambient Er Gen canon; the bridge-collapse scenario is the Constitution's canonical history-vs-momentum example (Article XXXIX.1)
